quiz_questions.question_text: What is centripetal acceleration?

options.text: Acceleration directed toward the center of a circular path that causes an object to follow a curved trajectory
Acceleration in the direction of motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration always points toward the center of the circle, continuously changing the direction of velocity to produce circular motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for centripetal acceleration?

options.text: a_c = v²/r, where v is the speed and r is the radius

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration = v²/r. A higher speed or smaller radius produces greater centripetal acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides the centripetal force for a car turning on a flat road?

options.text: Friction between the tires and the road

quiz_questions.explanation: On a flat road, static friction between the tires and road provides the inward (centripetal) force needed to change the car's direction during a turn.

quiz_questions.question_text: What provides the centripetal force for the Moon orbiting Earth?

options.text: Gravitational force between Earth and the Moon

quiz_questions.explanation: Gravity provides the centripetal force that keeps the Moon in orbit around Earth. The gravitational pull toward Earth continuously deflects the Moon's path into a circle.

quiz_questions.question_text: What happens to an object moving in a circle if the centripetal force suddenly disappears?

options.text: It moves in a straight line tangent to the circle at the point of release

quiz_questions.explanation: By Newton's first law, without net force the object continues in a straight line. This line is tangent to the circle at the release point.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object moves in a circle at constant speed. Is it accelerating?

options.text: Yes — the direction of velocity is continuously changing, which constitutes acceleration even though speed is constant
No — constant speed means no acceleration

quiz_questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity (a vector). In uniform circular motion, the direction changes continuously, so there is always a centripetal acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the formula for centripetal force?
quiz_questions.explanation: F_c = ma_c = m(v²/r) = mv²/r. The centripetal force is the net force directed toward the center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Is centripetal force a new type of force?

options.text: No — centripetal force is the name for whatever force(s) point toward the center: gravity, friction, tension, etc.
Yes — it is a fundamental force
Yes — it exists only in circular motion
No — it is the same as centrifugal force

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal force is not a new force — it is a role played by real forces (gravity, friction, tension, normal force) that happen to point toward the center of the circular path.

quiz_questions.question_text: A ball on a string moves in a horizontal circle. What force provides the centripetal force?
quiz_questions.explanation: The string's tension pulls the ball toward the center of the circle, providing the centripetal force needed for circular motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: As double the speed of an object in circular motion (keeping r constant), the centripetal force:

options.text: Quadruples — F_c = mv²/r, so doubling v makes F_c four times larger

quiz_questions.explanation: F_c = mv²/r. If v → 2v: F_c = m(2v)²/r = 4mv²/r. The force quadruples because of the v² dependence.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes uniform circular motion?

options.text: Motion along a circular path at constant speed, where the velocity direction changes continuously and the acceleration is always directed toward the center
Motion in a circle with increasing speed
Motion in a straight line at constant speed

quiz_questions.explanation: In uniform circular motion, speed is constant but velocity changes direction continuously. The acceleration (a = v²/r) and net force always point toward the center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centripetal acceleration?

options.text: The acceleration directed radially inward (toward the center of the circle) experienced by any object moving along a curved path, with magnitude v²/r
Acceleration tangent to the circular path

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal means 'center-seeking.' The acceleration has magnitude v²/r (or ω²r) and always points toward the center of curvature.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centripetal force?

options.text: The net force directed toward the center of a circular path, responsible for continuously changing the direction of an object's velocity — equal to mv²/r
A force that acts outward from the center
A new fundamental force unique to circular motion
The force that speeds up objects in a circle

quiz_questions.explanation: Centripetal force is the net inward force that causes circular motion. It is provided by real forces (gravity, tension, friction, etc.) directed toward the center.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes centrifugal force?

options.text: A fictitious (pseudo) force that appears to push objects outward in a rotating reference frame — it has no physical agent and exists only in non-inertial frames
A real force that pushes objects outward during circular motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Centrifugal force is not a real force — it is an apparent force experienced in rotating frames. In an inertial frame, objects move in straight lines; the 'outward push' is simply inertia.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes angular velocity?

options.text: The rate at which an object rotates or revolves, measured in radians per second (ω = 2π/T = 2πf)
The speed of an object moving in a circle
The rate of change of angular acceleration
The centripetal acceleration in angular form

quiz_questions.explanation: Angular velocity ω tells how fast the angle changes. It relates to linear speed by v = ωr and to period by ω = 2π/T.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the period of circular motion?

options.text: The time T for one complete revolution around the circular path
The speed divided by the radius
The number of revolutions per second

quiz_questions.explanation: Period T is the time for one full cycle. It relates to speed: T = 2πr/v, and to angular velocity: T = 2π/ω.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes frequency in circular motion?

options.text: The number of complete revolutions per unit time, f = 1/T, measured in hertz (Hz)
The radius of the circular path

quiz_questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = ω/(2π). It counts how many full circles per second. 1 Hz = 1 revolution per second.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes tangential velocity?

options.text: The component of velocity directed along the tangent to the circular path — its magnitude is the speed v = ωr, and its direction is continuously changing
Velocity directed away from the center
The rate at which speed changes

quiz_questions.explanation: Tangential velocity is perpendicular to the radius at every point. Its magnitude is constant in uniform circular motion, but its direction changes continuously.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a banked curve?

options.text: A curved road or track tilted at an angle so that the normal force provides a component of centripetal force, reducing reliance on friction
A curve on a flat road
A curve with extra friction coating
A curve designed only for slow speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: On a banked curve, the road is tilted so N sinθ contributes centripetal force. At the ideal banking angle, no friction is needed: tanθ = v²/(rg).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes non-uniform circular motion?

options.text: Circular motion where the speed changes — the object has both centripetal acceleration (toward center) and tangential acceleration (along the path)

quiz_questions.explanation: In non-uniform circular motion, the speed changes, producing tangential acceleration. The total acceleration is the vector sum of centripetal and tangential components.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car moves around a circular track of radius 50 m at 20 m/s. What is its centripetal acceleration?

options.text: 8 m/s² — a_c = v²/r = 400/50 = 8 m/s² toward the center

quiz_questions.explanation: a_c = v²/r = 20²/50 = 400/50 = 8 m/s² directed toward the center of the track.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 0.5 kg ball on a 1 m string moves in a horizontal circle at 4 m/s. What is the tension in the string?
quiz_questions.explanation: T = mv²/r = 0.5 × 4²/1 = 0.5 × 16 = 8 N. Tension provides the centripetal force.

quiz_questions.question_text: A satellite orbits Earth at radius r with speed v. If it moves to an orbit with radius 2r, how does the required centripetal acceleration change?

options.text: It becomes one-fourth — a_c = v²/r, and for a circular orbit v² = GM/r, so a_c = GM/r², which is inversely proportional to r²: doubling r gives a_c/4

quiz_questions.explanation: For a gravitational orbit: a_c = GM/r². At 2r: a_c = GM/(2r)² = GM/(4r²) = (1/4)(GM/r²). The centripetal acceleration is one-fourth.

quiz_questions.question_text: You swing a bucket of water over your head in a vertical circle. At the top of the circle, the water doesn't fall out. Why?

options.text: At the top, both gravity and the normal force from the bucket provide centripetal force. The water doesn't fall because the required centripetal acceleration (v²/r) exceeds or equals g, keeping the water pressed into the bucket
Surface tension holds the water in
Centrifugal force pushes the water into the bucket

quiz_questions.explanation: At the top: mg + N = mv²/r. If v²/r ≥ g, then N ≥ 0 (bucket still pushes on water). The water needs centripetal acceleration toward center, and gravity alone isn't enough — the bucket floor provides the rest.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 1000 kg car rounds a flat curve (r = 100 m). The coefficient of static friction is 0.6. What is the maximum safe speed? (g = 10 m/s²)
A fan blade rotates at 120 RPM. A point on the tip (r = 0.3 m) has what centripetal acceleration?

options.text: a_c = ω²r = (4π)² × 0.3 ≈ 47.4 m/s² — 120 RPM = 2 rev/s, ω = 2π × 2 = 4π rad/s

quiz_questions.question_text: A stone on a string moves in a vertical circle with radius 0.8 m. What is the minimum speed at the top for the string to remain taut?

options.text: √(gr) = √(9.8 × 0.8) ≈ 2.8 m/s — at minimum speed, tension = 0 and gravity alone provides centripetal force: mg = mv²/r

quiz_questions.explanation: At the top, minimum tension = 0: mg = mv²/r → v² = gr → v = √(9.8 × 0.8) = √7.84 ≈ 2.8 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: An engineer designs a banked curve for a highway with r = 200 m and target speed 25 m/s. What should the banking angle be? (g = 10 m/s²)
quiz_questions.explanation: tanθ = v²/(rg) = 625/2000 = 0.3125. θ = arctan(0.3125) ≈ 17.4°. At this angle, no friction is needed at the design speed.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child on a merry-go-round sits 2 m from the center. The ride completes one revolution in 4 seconds. What centripetal force does the 30 kg child experience?
A washing machine spin cycle rotates at 1200 RPM with drum radius 0.25 m. Compare the centripetal acceleration to g.

options.text: a_c = ω²r = (40π)² × 0.25 ≈ 3948 m/s² ≈ 403g — the acceleration is about 400 times gravity!
